Yep, that's right. The way it works is whenever you assign a value to an object in python using the dot notation, eg `user.name = 'Bob'`, under the hood it calls [\__setattr__](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__setattr__). In `BaseObject` I override that method [here](https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/zenpy/lib/api_objects/__init__.py#L18), to add the name of the modified attribute to the `_dirty_attributes` set.

There is one complication, in that when an object is initialized, inside the `__init__` method, attributes are assigned which populates the `_dirty_attributes` set. To work around that, I clear the set after instantiating the object [here](https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/zenpy/lib/mapping.py#L119) . That way, when the object is returned to the user the set is empty.

Once the user has made their modifications and are ready to update, the object needs to be converted to JSON. This is done by the [ZenpyObjectEncoder](https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/zenpy/lib/api.py#L23). The way this works is whenever the `json.dumps` method encounters a type that it doesn't understand, it calls the `default` method in the encoder class. If it is a Zenpy object, then I convert it to a Python dictionary using `to_dict(serialize=True)`, which only returns those attributes that are in the `_dirty_attributes` set, and the id. That way, only the modified attributes are converted and sent to Zendesk.

There is one final issue that could arise. Imagine a case where the user tries to update an object, but the update fails due to network instability. If the `_dirty_attributes` set was cleared before confirming that Zendesk had processed it, they would lose their changes. For this reason, I store the last object sent to Zendesk [here](https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/zenpy/lib/api.py#L70), and only when I know the update succeeded, do I clear the objects `_dirty_attributes` set [here](https://github.com/facetoe/zenpy/blob/master/zenpy/lib/api.py#L186).

That's basically how it works. Let me know if you have any questions!

Quick summary of issues I found, before I run out of time:

In ``core/server.py``, ``candidate_addresses()``:

```python
if self.mode == IP_SYSTEM_DEFAULT:
    candidates = addresses
```

Seems better, as otherwise it will only look at the first address.

In ``strategy/base.py``, ``open()``, catching all exceptions hides the real error, suspect we only need to catch ``LDAPSocketOpenError `` exception here.

In ``strategy/base.py, _open_socket()``, we try connecting to ``self.connection.server.address_info[0][4]``, which is incorrect (it seems to always return the first address in the list for every attempt), we should be trying to connect to ``address[4]`` (If I understand correctly).

This function call to ``self.connection.socket.connect`` returns the following exception from ``socket.py`` in Python, which I am still trying to understand, particularly as it seems to work on the first try with the same parameters:

```
TypeError: getsockaddrarg() takes exactly 2 parameters (4 given)
```

Will continue to investigate after my meeting.

I figured out why some people might be running into this issue.

tl;dr Recreate your virtualenv *after you've installed the python3.6-dev package* (or the python version your virtualenv is using).

Python 3.6 isn't supported in Ubuntu 16.04, so people may install it in different ways.

How I did it:

```
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:jonathonf/python-3.6
sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3.6 python3.6-dev
sudo apt remove python3-dev # to ensure the system's Python 3.5 wouldn't interfere

mkvirtualenv test --python=/usr/bin/python3.6
# now in the "test" virtualenv
pip install dlib
```

This gives the same results as what other commenters got.

Tracing this, I tried to install from dlib source using `python setup.py install` and got the same error.

Going to the cmake build dir `build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.6` I ran `make VERBOSE=1` to show compiler command lines.

The first target to fail uses the command line:
```
[ 72%] Building CXX object CMakeFiles/dlib_python.dir/src/dlib.cpp.o
/usr/bin/c++  -DDLIB_VERSION=19.15.99 -Ddlib_python_EXPORTS -I/home/joel/src/dlib/dlib/external/pybind11/include -I/home/joel/.virtualenvs/test/include/python3.6m -I/home/joel/src/dlib/dlib/..  -O3 -DNDEBUG -fPIC -fvisibility=hidden   -std=c++14 -DDLIB_NO_ABORT_ON_2ND_FATAL_ERROR -DDLIB_JPEG_SUPPORT -DDLIB_USE_BLAS -DDLIB_USE_LAPACK -DDLIB_USE_CUDA -DDLIB_PNG_SUPPORT -DDLIB_GIF_SUPPORT -mavx -Wreturn-type -std=gnu++11 -o CMakeFiles/dlib_python.dir/src/dlib.cpp.o -c /home/joel/src/dlib/tools/python/src/dlib.cpp
```

The issue is with `-I/home/joel/.virtualenvs/test/include/python3.6m` - which doesn't exist. The headers are in `/usr/include/python3.6m`.

**Solution: Create a new virtualenv after python3.6-dev (or equivalent) package is installed**

Why? When you create a virtualenv the include directories will be installed in the virtualenv *if they exist*.

So if you create a virtualenv, but don't have the headers for python (e.g. from `python3.6-dev`), then even if you install the headers in a system location afterwards, they won't be in the virtualenv and dlib will failed to build because of this.

Hope this helps!

I was particularly concerned that np.sum(matrix_2d, axis=()) (what we get
for a 2D input) might behave as np.sum(matrix_2d, axis=None) (scalar sum of
all elements), but it doesn't. np.sum(matrix_2d, axis=()) is simply a no-op.

But if you're asking how to simplify a statement that, according to the
constraints implied by the surrounding code, has a lot of no-op machinery,
I think it's worth considering changing the idiom, e.g.:

assert ref_slice.ndim == 2 and est_slice.ndim == 2
if not (np.any(np.all(ref_slice == 0, axis=1))) and not
(np.any(np.all(est_slice == 0, axis=1))):
("if neither ref_slice nor est_slice contain any columns that are all 0")

Also, I think this is equivalent to:

if np.all(np.any(ref_slice > 0, axis=1) and np.all(np.any(est_slice > 0,
axis=1))):
("if every column of both ref_slice and est_slice have at least one value
greater than zero")

I think the gloss of the first form is actually a little clearer, but
avoiding the negation makes the second expression clearer.

Finally, if you want to retain the version that retains the capacity to
accept arbitrary sample submatrices, I would recommend folding some part of
the duplicated idiom into a helper function, e.g.

def zero_valued_column_submatrices(tensor):
  """Returns a boolean vector of size tensor.shape[1] that is true if all
values in that slice are zero."""
  return np.all(np.sum(tensor, axis=tuple(range(2, tensor.ndim))) ==
0, axis=1)

then the original code becomes:

if (not np.any(zero_valued_column_submatrices(ref_slice) and
    not np.any(zero_valued_column_submatrices(est_slice)):

Tonight, let's see how to change *methods*.

--------

Telegram says:

> Added support for Foursquare venues: added ... the parameter *foursquare_type* to the sendVenue method.

> Added vCard support when sharing contacts: added the field *vcard* to ... the method sendContact.

The two methods, *sendVenue* and *sendContact*, are adjacent in the file `__init__.py`, within the `Bot` class. Let's look at them together. Here are the original:

```python
def sendVenue(self, chat_id, latitude, longitude, title, address,
              foursquare_id=None,
              disable_notification=None,
              reply_to_message_id=None,
              reply_markup=None):
    """ See: https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#sendvenue """
    p = _strip(locals())
    return self._api_request('sendVenue', _rectify(p))

def sendContact(self, chat_id, phone_number, first_name,
                last_name=None,
                disable_notification=None,
                reply_to_message_id=None,
                reply_markup=None):
    """ See: https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#sendcontact """
    p = _strip(locals())
    return self._api_request('sendContact', _rectify(p))
```

Changes are straight-forward:

```python
def sendVenue(self, chat_id, latitude, longitude, title, address,
              foursquare_id=None,
              foursquare_type=None,
              disable_notification=None,
              reply_to_message_id=None,
              reply_markup=None):
    """ See: https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#sendvenue """
    p = _strip(locals())
    return self._api_request('sendVenue', _rectify(p))

def sendContact(self, chat_id, phone_number, first_name,
                last_name=None,
                vcard=None,
                disable_notification=None,
                reply_to_message_id=None,
                reply_markup=None):
    """ See: https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#sendcontact """
    p = _strip(locals())
    return self._api_request('sendContact', _rectify(p))
```

Both are optional parameters, so default to `None`. There's nothing to change in the method body. `_strip(locals())` puts all method parameters other than `self` into a dict. `_rectify(p)` removes all `None` values before passing them to `self._api_request()`.

--------

Telegram says:

> Added the method sendAnimation, which can be used instead of sendDocument to send animations, specifying their duration, width and height.

Luckily, we have a lot of send* methods to copy from, e.g. sendDocument, sendVideo, sendVoice, etc. I use sendVideo as an example:

```python
def sendVideo(self, chat_id, video,
              duration=None,
              width=None,
              height=None,
              caption=None,
              parse_mode=None,
              supports_streaming=None,
              disable_notification=None,
              reply_to_message_id=None,
              reply_markup=None):
    """
    See: https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#sendvideo

    :param video: Same as ``photo`` in :meth:`telepot.Bot.sendPhoto`
    """
    p = _strip(locals(), more=['video'])
    return self._api_request_with_file('sendVideo', _rectify(p), 'video', video)
```

Making sendAnimation is just a matter of fixing names and matching method parameters with Telegram docs:

```python
def sendAnimation(self, chat_id, animation,
                  duration=None,
                  width=None,
                  height=None,
                  caption=None,
                  parse_mode=None,
                  disable_notification=None,
                  reply_to_message_id=None,
                  reply_markup=None):
    """
    See: https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#sendanimation

    :param video: Same as ``photo`` in :meth:`telepot.Bot.sendPhoto`
    """
    p = _strip(locals(), more=['animation'])
    return self._api_request_with_file('sendAnimation', _rectify(p), 'animation', animation)
```

Sending *files* takes some special handling. That's why I have to `_strip` the `animation` and pass it to `self._api_request_with_file()` outside of the rectified dict.

**Unfortunately, I wasn't aware of the "thumb" parameter, which must have been added at some earlier date. To take care of it, we have to modify `_api_request_with_file()` to handle one more file to be uploaded.**

--------

If the topic was not initially present when the consumer first started up, then you have to wait for the consumer to next do a metadata refresh before it will realize there's new topics. This is because the kafka protocol (by design) doesn't support the broker pushing an awareness of new topics. See the docs for `metadata_max_age_ms`, which defaults to every 5 minutes.

I don't see a way to work within the paradigm of the current code to de-duplicate log lines of the same operation on a 1-to-1 basis. So instead my idea is to remove duplicate group-by results in mloginfo.

First in logevent:
* Fix the namespace property for COMMAND lines
* Create the pattern for COMMAND lines too.
* (For some reason it seems inserts are excluded, so I don't include them either.)
```
diff --git a/mtools/util/logevent.py b/mtools/util/logevent.py
index ba74788..91e7cb0 100644
--- a/mtools/util/logevent.py
+++ b/mtools/util/logevent.py
@@ -392,7 +392,11 @@ class LogEvent(object):
 
         if op in self.log_operations:
             self._operation = op
-            self._namespace = split_tokens[self._datetime_nextpos + 2]
+            if op == "command":
+                self._namespace = split_tokens[self._datetime_nextpos + 2][0:-4] + split_tokens[self._datetime_nextpos + 7][1:-2]
+            else:
+                self._namespace = split_tokens[self._datetime_nextpos + 2]
+
 
 
 
@@ -405,6 +409,11 @@ class LogEvent(object):
             # trigger evaluation of operation
             if self.operation in ['query', 'getmore', 'update', 'remove'] or self.command in ['count', 'findandmodify']:
                 self._pattern = self._find_pattern('query: ')
+            elif self.command in ['update', 'delete']:
+                self._pattern = self._find_pattern('q: ')
+            elif self.command == 'find':
+                self._pattern = self._find_pattern('filter: ')
+
 
         return self._pattern
```

Then in mloginfo's queries section:
* If a group for "dbfoo.collfoo, op-type, pattern" exists and there is also a "dbfoo.collfoo, command-type, pattern" for the comparable legacy op name v.s. new command name then delete the older-style one. (The pairs are: "update" and "cmd.update", "remove" and "cmd.delete", "query" and "cmd.find")
* (I named the COMMAND lines ones "cmd.<command_type>" deliberately today, but it's debatable if we should do this or not.)

```
diff --git a/mtools/mloginfo/sections/query_section.py b/mtools/mloginfo/sections/query_section.py

 from collections import namedtuple

 try:

@@ -58,6 +59,9 @@ class QuerySection(BaseSection):
             if le.operation in ['query', 'getmore', 'update', 'remove'] or le.command in ['count', 'findandmodify', 'geonear']:
                 lt = LogTuple(namespace=le.namespace, operation=op_or_cmd(le), pattern=le.pattern, duration=le.duration)
                 grouping.add(lt)
+            elif le.command in ['find', 'update', 'delete']:
+                lt = LogTuple(namespace=le.namespace, operation="cmd." + op_or_cmd(le), pattern=le.pattern, duration=le.duration)
+                grouping.add(lt)
 
         grouping.sort_by_size()
 
@@ -105,6 +109,20 @@ class QuerySection(BaseSection):
         if self.mloginfo.args['sort'] in ['namespace', 'pattern']:
             reverse = False

+        trimmed_table_rows = [r for r in table_rows if ((r['namespace'], r['operation'], r['pattern']) not in groups_to_del_set)]
```

        def _get_blank_slide_layout(pres):

        def copy_slide(pres,pres1,index):
             source = pres.slides[index]

             for shp in source.shapes:

Thanks for the note! 

I'll address the specific packages first, then the broader question. 

1. lightgbm- we use this for quantile regression
2. h5py- we use this when saving some models
3. dill- yep, not needed if you're not saving models. but saving models is a major use case of auto_ml. if you're only looking for batch processing accuracy, i'd probably recommend [auto-sklearn](https://github.com/automl/auto-sklearn) or [xcessiv](https://github.com/EpistasisLab/tpot) or [tpot](https://github.com/EpistasisLab/tpot). They're better about doing things like ensembling that work well for batch predictions, but add a layer of complexity that's not as obvious of a win in production environments, and make the analytics from models much more difficult to interpret. 
4. tensorflow - we removed this from the list of requirements some releases ago, along with keras. still usable, but now the user has to install it. 
5. sklearn-deap2 - used for our grid search replacement, evolutionary-algorithm search

In general, I think it's important for someone to be able to `pip install auto_ml` and have everything they need to run all the major use cases already installed. Hard drive space is cheap, and outside of tensorflow, most of these packages don't use up much space. But the developer time cost of debugging why something isn't working is expensive. 

One of the goals of this project is to have machine learning "just work". It seems contrary to that goal to not install the packages people need for some pretty major use cases. The packages we rely on are also, unfortunately, not always the best engineering projects. It's been a major time sink for me updating auto_ml as the external packages cut new releases that break their APIs without warning. So specifying which versions of packages we support and installing those versions automatically saves everyone a big headache. 

I see the frustration, and I love getting suggestions, but I think the current approach best suits the aims of this project. 

1. Hard disk space is cheap
2. All of the packages you mentioned (with the exception of tensorflow, which is a beast) are pretty light on memory
3. Developer time is expensive, and I don't want people wondering why we didn't install necessary packages automatically
4. Making ML as turnkey as possible is one of auto_ml's big goals. 

Let me know if there's something major I didn't consider. Again, thanks for the feedback- would love to hear what other ideas you have.

I've been giving a second thought to the patch during the weekend and running some more tests. The case is that `_is_specialization()` is trying to use `issubclass()` over a Union. This can happen when you provide a `cls` that is not `generic_class`, in my case the issue is only happening to me when using (Class)?AssistedBuilders, which make the comparison with `Union` fail. Then, the `issubclass(ClassAssistedBuilder[T], Union)`, fails since those are not classes.

I have updated the patch by making sure that both objects being compared are their types instead of themselves if those are instances. For the purposes of finding an whether `cls` is and specialization of `generic_class`, comparing their types should be equivalent.

I'm happy to cleanup a bit the code (maybe the two versions of `_is_specialization()` could be merged) and adding a test case that reproduces the assisted builder I was using.

the default ajax [modifies the response ](Receiving_binary_data_in_older_browsers)
There are a few options
- set the responseType to blob and use that instead
- use `fetch(url).then(res=>res.blob()).then(saveAs(blob, 'filename'))`
- override mimetype and construct it as you are doing (see link)
- don't use jQuery.ajax (it can only handle string & json - not binary like buffer and blob)
- use download attribute instead

or better yet if you have control over the server
- Don't use FileSaver at all use content-disposition attachment header (you will get better support in safari and older browser)

Flask-Allows follows the convention of providing an `init_app` for delayed application initialization.  If the app is provided in the Allows constructor, it calls init app for you, otherwise you must call it later.

Both of these calls need to be made only once. I prefer application factories (e. g. there's a function that creates the actual application instance for me), so I end up calling both. This is also the way that sh4nks/flaskbb sets up Allows. The Allows instance is created in the extensions module and then init app is called when the application is created. I'd recommend taking a peek at that project for a concrete example.

2.2 valid mail address would always have count = 2 = true

So in both cases of 2. the count() part would be superfluous.

Only if there is such a problem with filter_var, this check *might* be useful.
I've looked at the php reference and found this: http://php.net/manual/de/function.filter-var.php#112492
The user states that `"this is v@lid!"@example.com` could be a valid mail address, but the filter function doesn't accept it. So the second part of the first if clause (count($mail)) would cause a `return false` although the mail address is valid, if the filter function would not have already stopped that string...

Please excuse me: I tend to talk too much :-)
In short my conclusion is that the count($mail) is not needed.

And if it is really not needed, I would put the explode() **after** the filter function since that call would be unneeded if the filter function already eliminates a malformed mail address. You only have to explode $email if you compare the last part with the list.

<hr>

So now that I read that `"this is v@lid!"@example.com` would be a possible mail address, the code should better read

~~~
  if(filter_var($email, FILTER_VALIDATE_EMAIL) === false) {
    return false;
  }
  $mail = explode("@", $email);
  return !in_array(strtolower(end($mail)), array(...));
}
~~~
Otherwise it might be possible to trick this function with `"evil@msn.com"@somespamhoster.com`

Since the $mail variable is only needed once in the code above you can consider using directly `return !in_array(strtolower(end(explode("@", $email))), array(...));`. You have to weight readability against performance here.

Thinking about it some time longer I think it is a good habit to keep small libraries as fast as they can be, if they still are readable. Since we are talking about less than half a dozen lines of code I really would drop the $mail variable.

Ok, another update on this issue.
So it turns out there _*was*_ already two ways to set the `automatic_options` config on an application-level.

First there is this way:
```python
app = Sanic(__name__)
CORS(app, automatic_options=True)

@app.route('/test')
async def get_test(request):
    return text('hello world')
```
Using this method, this sets up standard CORS headers on ALL requests (you don't need to use `@cross_origin(app)` on your routes), and also applies the options in kwargs to all requests (in the case in this example it applies `automatic_options` to all requests).

Secondly, if you don't want to use the `CORS(app)` extension controller, (if you just want to use `@cross_origin(app)` decorator on specific routes), then you can use the `app.config` method:

```python
app = Sanic(__name__)
app.config['CORS_AUTOMATIC_OPTIONS'] = True

@app.route('/test', methods=["GET", "OPTIONS"])
@cross_origin(app)
async def get_test(request):
    return text('hello world')
```
Sanic-CORS has the ability to read any config variable stored in the `app.config` container, that has a key starting with `"CORS_"`. In this case, it sets `automatic_options` to true for all routes _including_ those using the `@cross_origin` decorator. Notice however you will need to add "OPTIONS" to the allowed methods passed to the Sanic `@app.route()` decorator to keep the Sanic router happy when using `automatic_options` in this manner.

Finally, this can `app.config` method also be used with the `CORS()` extension controller like so:
```python
app = Sanic(__name__)
app.config['CORS_AUTOMATIC_OPTIONS'] = True
CORS(app)
@app.route('/test')
async def get_test(request):
    return text('hello world')
```

Finally, I found a bug in the `get_cors_options()` function in Sanic-CORS, it was not finished being ported from Flask-CORS properly. That bug caused `app.config` options to be ignored in Sanic-CORS, so those last two examples above wouldn't work if you tried to use those snippets. I have released a new version v0.5.4.2 that fixes that.

You could do this with flask [blueprints](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/blueprints/). I've yet to update the documentation, but you would initialize each agent as a blueprint like so:


```
# project/assistant_1/webhook.py

from flask import Blueprint
from flask_assistant import Assistant

blueprint = Blueprint('assist', __name__, url_prefix='/assist')
assist = Assistant(blueprint=blueprint)
```

```
# project/assistant_2/webhook.py

from flask import Blueprint
from flask_assistant import Assistant

blueprint = Blueprint('assist_2', __name__, url_prefix='/assist_2')
assist = Assistant(blueprint=blueprint)
```

then in app.py or wherever your flask app is created:

```
# project/app.py

from project import assistant_1, assistant_2

app = Flask(__name__)
register_blueprints(app)

def register_blueprints(app):
    app.register_blueprint(assistant_1.webhook.blueprint)
    app.register_blueprint(assistant_2.webhook.blueprint)
```

For each assistant object, you would just point API.AI to the url_prefix given to desired blueprint.
Agent 1 --> https://6e996943.ngrok.io/assist
Agent 2 --> https://6e996943.ngrok.io/assist_2

As fast workaround, we could add `path_info` to message in Daphne, instead of doing this resolution in Channels. We can later use this to filter messages in Channels routing by using `path_info` instead of `path`.
But [ASGI message spec](http://channels.readthedocs.io/en/stable/asgi/www.html#connection) doesn't speak of `path_info`, instead we should have `path` and `root_path` set in messages on `websocket.connect` channel. By the way, why `root_path` doesn't appear in the spec on `websocket.receive` and `websocket.disconnect` channels ?

Also, another consequence for Channels is that urls reverse should not work in websocket consumers, as `set_script_prefix` from `django.urls` is never used (which is done by `ASGIHandler` for message on `http.request`, as in Django `WSGIHandler`). Note that I didn't check this for real.

Fix this (for Daphne) should be easy, we only have to add `root_path` in websockets messages. For Channels, if we want to keep the routing filters as generic as it is now, path filtering should be done with `path_info` instead of `path`, which is consistent with Django [`BaseHandler`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/core/handlers/base.py#L115) resolver (which use `path_info`).

the added [test](https://github.com/web2py/pydal/pull/194/files#diff-3b2d9f3a86b72f767916790fb46cc94eR279) is quite... uhm, screwy.
Even if you can succesfully insert something specified as a sequence/identity/call_whatever_you_like you're putting yourself in harm's way because there are additional steps to take to ensure things won't get screwed afterwards, such as trying to insert a row that happens to hold "1234" as the next value whose "place" has been already filled up.
On the top of my head, for example, postgresql needs resyncing of the sequence of the serial, and mssql needs special statements to wrap things up.

<tl;dr> we AREN'T going to support in any way inserting a specific value to a table created by web2py, since we already ruled out that it's a silly thing to do (and, as explained here, there are so much more things to do to support it in a safe and standardized way). 
That being said, we want to be nicer to people who are using legacy tables with an 'id' column that is an integer BUT is not autogenerated. So, ok for the fix, not ok for the test-case.

Is it possible your performance degradation comes from no longer operating in true-fp16 mode?  I went back to your earlier issue and was reminded that you compile with MSHADOW_USE_PASCAL=1.  @piiswrong made the decision that mxnet users going forward would only have pseudo-fp16 mode available.  We discussed having the policy set by an environment variable, but decided that the precision and range issues of true-fp16 were best avoided entirely.  I implemented this with PR https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/7527.  Try building against an mxnet version earlier than Aug. 23rd if you want to explore this explanation to your problems.

Btw, if Gu load libgfortran and things start going faster, but cpu utilization goes down, this doesn’t imply that libgfortran is “limiting cpu usage”. Mxnet, like just about every multithreaded system, does not run with perfect concurrency at every point in the execution process. That doesn’t mean that there isn’t a bottleneck somewhere (ie file IO, memory cache misses) that could be improved, but I don’t think it necessarily points to libgfortran forcibly slowing things down.

You may be able to use `fs.mkdtemp` (the asynchronous version) instead, in which case you need to make sure that you handle the asynchronous part right.

For example change something like this:

```
var bar = fs.mkdtempSync('/tmp/BAR-')
doSomething(bar);
```

to look more like this:

```
fs.mkdtemp('/tmp/BAR-', function (bar) {
    doSomething(bar);
})
```

Since traitlets already has a framework for descriptor setup and still suport Python<3.6, we are probably limited to utilizing `otherargs` in `type.__new__` (for what I'm not sure). However something noteworthy is that PEP 487 implements a subclass setup phase, while traitlets only implements an "owner class" setup phase in `class_init`.

Being able to distinguish between an owner class, and any other subclass remains significant, for traitlets because there's a need for inheritance enforcement, but it does suggest that it subclass setup may be a useful feature. In other words we may want a `subclass_init` method for the same purposes it serves in PEP 487.

At first I thought this might be related to https://github.com/boto/botocore/issues/1248 which is the only confirmed leak I know of. 

However, looking into this it seems to me that this is related to the client/resource object. That being said this isn't a memory leak, the reason you're seeing the ramp up in memory is that each time you create a session/client we have to go to disk to load the JSON models representing the service, etc. There's so much contention on a single file it takes ~20-30 seconds to even instantiate all 100 session/clients, considering each session has its own cache I'm actually not all that surprised by these memory usage numbers. 

I would suggest doing something like this:
```python
def run_pool(size):
    ts = []
    session = boto3.Session()
    for x in range(size):
        s3 = session.resource('s3')
        t = Boto3Thread(s3)
        t.start()
        ts.append(t)
    return ts
```
This way you only instantiate one session, and can actually leverage the caching that the session provides to instantiate all 100 resource objects to give to each thread.

It is not a very promising candidate for merging back into botocore.

The changes are numerous, and the changes needed to make botocore functional within asyncio make it non-functional outside asyncio.   I expect it to be a seperate product indefinitely, with a parralel API, meaning an API as similar as possible within the asyncio constraints.

The best way to do this now is to just unregister the handler that does the decoding:

```
>>> import botocore.session
>>> from botocore import handlers
>>> s = botocore.session.get_session()
>>> s.unregister('after-call.ec2.GetConsoleOutput', handlers.decode_console_output)
>>> ec2 = s.create_client('ec2')
>>> ec2.get_console_output(InstanceId='i-123')
{u'InstanceId': 'i-123', u'Output': 'MjAxNS8wOS8xNy...base64 encoded content...')}
```

Very few services have a `required` field in the output. None of the official SDKs do any sort of validation on responses, so it would be ignored anyway (static sdks necessarily validate types, but that's it). Some community sdks have actually [been bitten](https://github.com/boto/botocore/issues/629) in the past when those attributes were applied incorrectly.

I would recommend that you treat all response items as optional, which is what we do (implicitly). You could potentially still respect `required` in responses, but you might run into issues like the one I listed.

I believe the original design of colander (as extensively documented!) is reasonable and correct. I also believe that using `colander.drop` in `default` is reasonable and correct. What I think is wrong is that one specific value of `default`, `colander.drop`, affects *de*serialization. I think that was an inadvertent change caused by shared code and a hole in the test suite.

I was able to find a workaround—not based on `deferred()` and `bind()` as it turns out—so I'm not stuck. But I still think this is a bug worth fixing.

Here's a fix I was considering but didn't try. `serialize()` and `deserialize()` both call `_impl()` (in both `Mapping` and `Sequence`). `_impl()` then checks `default`. I was thinking that it might work to pass a second parameter into `_impl()`, the name of the attribute that should be checked: `"default"` for `serialize()`,  `"missing"` for `deserialize()`. I'm very new to colander, though—just started with it last week—so I don't know enough to know whether or not that actually makes sense.

@remram44 2 questions:

1. Are you experiencing an issue currently if you `pipenv install <some torch wheel>`?
2. If pipenv were directly calling `setup.py egg_info`, why would it be problematic?

As a sidenote, there is a reason why we distinguish between editable and non-editable installs. Non-editable URLs are not deeply resolved, which means `egg_info` won't get called by running `pipenv lock`.  These are explicitly excluded from being passed to the resolver.

@JulienPalard it's closed because of a few reasons:

1. Pipfiles and `setup.py` files are not really meant to be kept in sync, per se, I think there have been a bunch of articles linked discussing the details but tl;dr `Pipfile` is roughly equivalent to a top level `requirements.txt`
2. If you want your project (say it's the current directory) to have its `setup.py` resolved into the managed virtualenv, the workflow would just be `pipenv install -e .` -- this puts a single entry into your `Pipfile` (the top level root) and the resolved dependency graph into your `Pipfile.lock` including the resolved `install_requires`. If you want to update the virtualenv with the latest contents due to a changed `install_requires`, you'd have to run `pipenv update` which is the same as `pipenv lock && pipenv sync` in the latest version.

Hope this is helpful!

> Regarding "passing a parameter through several calls", do you mean that namespace_packages is passed on from find to find_packages_iter? If so, how else should it be implemented?

Indeed, I don't see a better way. And perhaps I'm being too sensitive here. I'll drop that concern.

> I was under the impression that in current versions, you don't have to list implicit namespace packages in namespace_packages (the setup parameter). So there would be no duplication, right?

You may be right, though I haven't looked into it deeply. I do know that if you want the `__init__.py` file for backward compatibility, you do need to declare the namespace package in setup.py. The tools do need some way to know all namespace packages in order to generate the appropriate `-nspkg.pth` files on older Pythons, but for pure PEP 420 packages, that parameter may be unnecessary.

> It [`__nspkg__`] could even be named __init__.py!

It can't because the presence of a `__init__.py` precludes the initialization or inclusion of that package as a namespace package.

> Serious question: Is there any difference in actually accessing a package (via import etc) between an __init__.py package and a namespace package? Maybe there's a difference of intent. And obviously one has an __init__.py file that may limit access to the enclosed modules and objects. But if you use an __init__.py file that gives access to all enclosed, this would be imported and called just like the same package with no __init__.py file (ie: a namespace package), no? Why are there even these two concepts?

Yes. There's some good reading [in pep 402](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0402/#the-problem) and the second paragraph of the [PEP 420 spec](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0420/#id24) specifically says namespace packages cannot contain a `__init__.py`. This behavior allows a package to be a "regular" package and exclude the possibility of other modules appearing in the same namespace.

> Maybe I'm missing something, but it just seems to me that namespace packages need to be rethought.

For better or worse, this design is the one we must work with in the build tools. The design won't change until at least Python 3.7, and before that happens, the issue will need to be well-understood and a cogent solution presented, probably in a PEP.

> PEP 420 is not well thought out, especially for tools needing to deduce package structure from the file system

Perhaps. The PEP focuses on the import system and not build/packaging systems. The word "discover" appears twice and only in reference to the import system. I wonder how other languages handle this condition - how does Java or Perl (the two languages referenced in the PEPs) deal with package discovery during the build process? Does that provide any inspiration? I know nothing about Perl, but my experience with Java makes me think they're just very explicit about it, not dissimilar to the proposal in #915, or even more explicit without any discovery, such as `setup(packages['myns.foo'])`.

> debhelper for Python distributions actually strips the __init__.py for namespace packages that are declared as such with namespace_packages in the setup call (wanting to encourage PEP 420 I guess, and avoid file conflicts)

Actually, this behavior is baked into setuptools and pip as well. In setuptools, it happens with `setup.py install --single-version-externally-managed`, which is probably what the deb helper is invoking and what pip invokes. In addition to stripping the `__init__.py`, it also adds a `-nspkg.pth` file which provides backward compatibility in older Pythons. In [28.3](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/history.html#v28-3-0), Setuptools tried relying entirely on PEP 420 for Python 3.3+, but in [version 31](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/history.html#v31-0-0), this change was reverted as the `-nspkg.pth` is necessary to override PEP-420 behavior for namespace packages which have the `__init__.py` for compatibility reasons.

> The result was that developers working on other distributions of ours, with packages in that same namespace package, who set up their PYTHONPATH to point at the distribution they were hacking on, got the dev tool broken since Python couldn't see its PEP 420 package

The release of Setuptools 31 addresses this use case through the use of `setup.py develop` (invoked by `pip install -e`), which should be preferred over PYTHONPATH hacks.

And I should point out that if you want to support Python 3.3+ only in your namespace package and rely on PEP 420, that is possible today - you just can't use `find_packages` but must specify your packages explicitly in setup.py.

>`__nspkg__` would only be present in the package dir in the distribution, not installed when doing setup.py install, so just a marker for the purpose of getting find_packages working

Yes, though I hadn't thought through it too deeply. The marker would have to be included in sdists. To be conservative, it would be nice if it were not installed to the environment, to prevent its abuse for other purposes.

For this kind of question, it's important to separate two different concepts:

1. Optional submodules, where a given component installs into a shared package module hierarchy. This doesn't require namespace packages at all, it just requires what @matham describes in https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/895#issuecomment-268722067

In 3.3+ and when using `importlib2` that will work standalone (since `__init__.py` files become optional), while in 3.2 and earlier it will require `farm` or `farm.deps` as a dependency that provides the relevant `__init__.py` files.

2. Namespace packages, which allow a package to be split across multiple directories by having more than one entry in `__path__`.

Python 3.3+ treats any directory without an `__init__.py` file as a potential part of a namespace package automatically, while the `declare_namespace` functionality in setuptools lets you request the generation of a boilerplate `__init__.py` that takes care of setting `__path__` appropriately based on a search of `sys.path`.

The fact that these are separate questions means that a non-namespace package may have namespace packages as children, as long as it includes the necessary `__path__` expansion code in its `__init__.py`: https://docs.python.org/3/library/pkgutil.html#pkgutil.extend_path

The trick with doing that is that you don't tell *setuptools* that it is a namespace package, you instead publish it as a normal self-contained package that happens to manipulate its own `__path__` attribute.

However, if all you want is optional submodules, then you don't need to use namespace packages at all - just make the submodules depend on the parent package that they install into, and let the parent package take care of installing the necessary `__init__.py` files.

Hi @vivk89, and thanks for reaching out. Your code creates a python [`Partition` object](http://pykafka.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/partition.html) in memory and does not communicate with Kafka brokers after the initial connection set up by `KafkaClient`. It sounds like you might be trying to add partitions to an existing topic on your kafka cluster. For this, you'll want to use the kafka [command-line tools](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#quickstart_createtopic) instead of pykafka.

The Metadata requests will keep the clients up to date.

Take a look here: https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/A+Guide+To+The+Kafka+Protocol#AGuideToTheKafkaProtocol-Partitioningandbootstrapping

> How can the client find out which topics exist, what partitions they have, and which brokers currently host those partitions so that it can direct its requests to the right hosts? This information is dynamic, so you can't just configure each client with some static mapping file. Instead all Kafka brokers can answer a metadata request that describes the current state of the cluster: what topics there are, which partitions those topics have, which broker is the leader for those partitions, and the host and port information for these brokers.

> In other words, the client needs to somehow find one broker and that broker will tell the client about all the other brokers that exist and what partitions they host. This first broker may itself go down so the best practice for a client implementation is to take a list of two or three urls to bootstrap from. The user can then choose to use a load balancer or just statically configure two or three of their kafka hosts in the clients.

> The client does not need to keep polling to see if the cluster has changed; it can fetch metadata once when it is instantiated cache that metadata until it receives an error indicating that the metadata is out of date. This error can come in two forms: (1) a socket error indicating the client cannot communicate with a particular broker, (2) an error code in the response to a request indicating that this broker no longer hosts the partition for which data was requested.

I've been pondering this as I changed the [Python version](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/pull/3307).

**Update**

I think these two things are worth solving:

1. Remove all the logic around destroyed segments and synapses
   - Stop forcing the Python implementation to do this.
2. Stop creating a new vector every time we call segmentsForCell or synapsesForSegment.
   - Perf!

But I don't think we should use pointers for identity. Here's the main reason: we would want the pointers to be const, but we'd want the Connections to modify the thing that they point to. That would be a very weird API, passing a `const Synapse*` into `connections.updateSynapsePermanence()` and having it modify the Synapse. The method would be receiving a Synapse* that it's not allowed to use, then it would go and find a non-const version of the same pointer, or const_cast. Either way, gross.

Instead, we should stick to having a `Segment` and `Synapse` type that acts as a "name", while the `SegmentData` and `SynapseData` act as the "data".

To get rid of destroyed Segments and Synapses, we'll move the data structs into a flat list. Rather than using the cell + segmentIndex as a path, we'll use the index in the flat list, and we'll have a reverse-lookup flat list (like we do today). When destroying a segment, we'll remove the `Segment` from the `CellData`, and we'll mark the `SegmentData` in the flat list as destroyed. We'll use the same process with synapses.

Rather than storing the segment's per-cell index, we'll store its birthday (an iteration number or just "this is the nth segment that has ever existed on this cell"). The correct sort order for segments on a cell will be oldest-to-youngest. That way we don't have to update indices when deleting a segment / synapse.

A Segment and a Synapse are each essentially just an integer, but we'll include cell (and segment) to make them groupable.

Now `synapsesForSegment` and `segmentsForCell` can just return a const reference to the existing vector, and the "destroyed" logic will be much simpler and won't affect the algorithm, so it can be totally removed from the Python.

I downloaded the latest db and ran through the code. These are 2 different things. The green 'token' comes from ProjectStatus and reflects the number of TestRuns which ended in 'expected' state. When creating a TestRun, TestJob is used as a base. But the status from LAVA is discarded as it's sometimes misleading (or irrelevant). So TestRun is considered 'completed' when SQUAD was able to retrieve something from it and the state looks sane. 

On the right, the status displayed comes directly from LAVA. The red X comes from the fact that LAVA returned error at some point. It could be while submitting job or when retrieving results. The error can also come from the job status. 

To sum up these 2 items have nothing to do with each other. I wonder how to fix the confusion. I don't want to rely on LAVA statuses as they sometimes seem a bit too aggressive. On the other hand it would simplify LAVA job handling (incomplete lava jobs would be simply ignored).

Hi @msfeghouli.

You can define a service that uses `role_label` selectors inside in order to route your requests to replicas. For example:

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  labels:
    application: spilo
    role: replica
    version: patronidemo
  name: patronidemo-repl
  namespace: default
spec:
  ports:
  - name: postgresql
    port: 5432
    protocol: TCP
    targetPort: 5432
  selector:
    role: replica
    cluster-name: patronidemo
  type: ClusterIP

```

Given that in Patroni the `role_label` is configured to `role` (default) and the cluster label to `cluster-name` (also default) it will make a new service called `patronidemo-repl` that will route your request to only pods with of cluster `patronidemo` with a role `replica`.

Also take a look a the https://github.com/zalando-incubator/postgres-operator project, that does a lot of work for you, allowing you to run your PostgreSQL clusters by providing a simple manifest like [this](https://github.com/zalando-incubator/postgres-operator/blob/master/manifests/minimal-postgres-manifest.yaml)

@DotNetPart Imho you should give the operator a try, this should give you less problems initially. You can then dive into the statefulset/pod configuration we use there for Spilo/Patroni to understand what is happening.

Okay, looks like I should have referenced this issue in my pull request to link this issue in. Since I didn't do that, here's the link:

https://github.com/zalando/connexion/pull/77

If what you want is [polymorphism](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/3.0.1.md#schemaComposition), then there is a feature in OpenAPI v3 which allows the API to accept multiple types of objects with (using oneOf), but each type of object must still have a schema (i.e. be typed). OpenAPI v3 support is in work for connexion at the moment (https://github.com/zalando/connexion/pull/621).

Having type information is required by Swagger 2.0 https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#parameter-object
Connexion implements the OpenAPI spec, so your request is simply not possible with Swagger 2.0.

Good question. This is actually a design choice (that needs some discussion). Here is the explanation (I'll assume RowMajor in the following discussion, and x_1 is the feature maps and x_2 the filters):
In Eigen, only the layout NHWC for x_1 and WHCN for x_2 are supported.
While, cuDNN supports NHWC or NCHW for x_1, and NHWC or NCHW for x_2, but only **NCHW can get the maximal performance.**

The above conditions imply that there will be at least one layout transformation happening, either for CPU version and GPU version.

Considering that layout transformation is usually equally fast on CPU and GPU, I design it to let it happen only f or CPU convolution. Reasons are as follows:
1. CPU runs convolution must slower than GPU, so the relative slowdown caused by layout transformation will be less significant;
2. The code is easier to implement on CPU :)

Let me know what you think about!

MR `shardCount` X means that the overall input for the `map` stage will be divided by X shards.

An MR shard is a unit of work that is processed sequentially (triggered by one or more sequential taskqueue tasks, each of which is called a `slice`).

Multiple shards can work in parallel to each other, but that **depends on** your queue settings and the instance configuration rather than control it.

It's reasonable to think you can do it.  I don't know exactly what the `mock.MagicMock` class does to mock another class, but generally, it's possible to pickle a class except in certain cases.  You might want to try changing one of the settings:

```
>>> import dill
>>> dill.settings
{'recurse': False, 'byref': False, 'protocol': 2, 'fmode': 0}
```

Setting `'recurse'` to `True` will change how `dill` attempts to pickle the `globals` dict, and by setting `'byref'` to True, it will pickle the class by reference instead of storing the class object itself.  Trying again with combinations of the above may find success for you.

You can also turn on pickle 'tracing', to see what `dill` is doing.  That often helps identity the "bad" objects within the object you want to serialize.  "F1: …" and similar, is an object starting the process of being dumped.  The corresponding `#F1` is the object finishing the dump.  You can see `dill` pickles recursively.  This trace will change depending on the settings you use (see above).

```
>>> dill.detect.trace(True)
>>> dill.dumps(lambda x:x)
F1: <function <lambda> at 0x1036c1668>
F2: <function _create_function at 0x10359f230>
# F2
Co: <code object <lambda> at 0x103345630, file "<stdin>", line 1>
T1: <type 'code'>
F2: <function _load_type at 0x10359f140>
# F2
# T1
# Co
D1: <dict object at 0x103080168>
# D1
D2: <dict object at 0x10367e168>
# D2
# F1
'\x80\x02cdill.dill\n_create_function\nq\x00(cdill.dill\n_load_type\nq\x01U\x08CodeTypeq\x02\x85q\x03Rq\x04(K\x01K\x01K\x01KCU\x04|\x00\x00Sq\x05N\x85q\x06)U\x01xq\x07\x85q\x08U\x07<stdin>q\tU\x08<lambda>q\nK\x01U\x00q\x0b))tq\x0cRq\rc__builtin__\n__main__\nh\nNN}q\x0etq\x0fRq\x10.'
```

Lastly, there are some decent diagnostic tools in `dill.detect`.  I use them when the trace is not sufficient to understand what's going on.  Feel free to post the "pickle trace" here.

The major difference is whether or not `dump` does a "shallow" pickling of `globals` or a "deep" pickling of `globals`.  This flag is also relevant for `dill.source.globalvars` and other items in `dill.source`, where the choice comes down to whether one wants to do a "shallow" pass at getting the `globals` or a "deep" one (using recursion). I started with `shallow=False` to have the code recursively find the referred-to `globals`, then didn't like the name, so opted for `recurse=True`.

Interesting -- I love the result of this (a clean, declarative way of defining permissions), but I don't love the implementation. It feels a bit too meta-y for what is basically a simple CRUD app and makes this bit of code unapproachable to a lot of developers (e.g. me -- I am not smart enough to program with metaclasses).

Also looks like tests are failing because (I suspect) Django changed something about how the class-based views' metaclasses worked as of 1.8, which causes some kind of conflict with the new permission metaclass. The fact that it introduces that level of complexity and surface area is, in my opinion, validation that the juice doesn't seem worth the squeeze here.

But like I said, I love the result -- I just wonder if there is a way to do it that's a little more approachable from a complexity standpoint.

If the solve for getting tests passing is straightforward, I'd reconsider -- but it really feels like a non metaclass-based solution should be possible.

Regardless of where we land on this -- THANK YOU for the thoughtful PR!

If you add an explicit role, the documentation states this about policies:

> Names of AWS managed IAM policies or IAM policy documents or SAM Policy Templates that this function needs, which should be appended to the default role for this function. If the Role property is set, this property has no meaning.

So, it would seem, you cannot combine an explicit Role with explicit Policies in the template. Adding a role will mean that the policies section is not used.

However, note that it does hint that policies added in the template will be appended to the default role that SAM creates. So, if you do not add a Role, and SAM creates one for you, it will apply any policies.

Usage is as easy as

```python
###################################################

import os, sys

c = {

        {'name':'bar', 'data':['three', 'four', 'five'], "baz":"def", "date":"1132"},        
    ]
}

#
# loop over all test files and process them
#
for p in ["test_template_edge_case_1.docx", "test_template_edge_case_2.docx", "test_template_features.docx"]:

        i = os.path.join("test_files", e, "in", p)
        o = os.path.join("test_files", e, "out", p)
        d = Document(i)
        d.save(o, context=c, engine=e)

###################################################
```

@shinmog 

Brian and I actually had a chance to chat about this at Ignite. This is a tricky one given the current thought process for linking. Personally, all of my use cases involve pulling in _everything_ and then linking. Given that, I would prefer to not have to make a call to `link_references()`, I would want it to just do the linking when everything is pulled in automatically. 

That being said, there are valid use cases for not doing this. Notably when the config is very large. I lean on the side of leave non-linkable elements as stings. This feels more pythonic to me than raising a fatal error just because a single element in a tree of hundreds or even thousands of elements could not be linked.

I think it fair to say, not everyone is going to use this new `link_references()` method and those that will, are likely to be okay with some type checking (contradicting myself on what is pythonic ;) ).

Also, as you demonstrated, returning tuples is pretty fast!  Benchmarks below use `cytoolz`:
```python
In [1]: import cytoolz as itz

In [2]: from cytoolz import curried

In [3]: %timeit itz.count(itz.partition(10, 20 * list(range(20))))
4.33 µs ± 89.2 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100000 loops each)

In [4]: %timeit itz.count(bytearray(x) for x in itz.partition(10, 20 * list(range(20))))
36.4 µs ± 1.34 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)

In [3]: %timeit itz.count(pipe(itz.partition(10, 20 * list(range(20))), curried.map(bytearray)))
47.2 µs ± 968 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```
Here we see that casting to `bytearray` is the slowest operation.  Using `curried.map` adds some overhead (about 30%) in the last example compared to a generator comprehension.

Actually, glyphsLib uses UCD3.2 only for “compression”: almost all entries in GlyphData can be inferred from the Unicode character data, and UCD3.2 is predictably available in all Python builds whereas later UCD versions may or may not be installed. Therefore, glyphsLib tries to infer the properties from UCD3.2, but of course there is an exception list to handle glyphs where the UCD look-up gives a different result than what is in GlyphsData.xml. In other words, using a different UCD version would make absolutely no difference for this bug. For example, glyphsLib already returns the correct result for `longlowtonecomb-nko`.

The problem is something else. Currently, glyphsLib assumes that Glyphs source files actually use the Glyphs-specific glyph names if one has defined. However, it looks like Noto N’Ko doesn’t follow the naming convention. To be more resilient, GlyphsLib should implement a work-around to handle fonts that don’t follow the Glyphs naming conventions. See https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/issues/232.

Okay to continue on the slack discussion let me be a bit clear on what are allowed and what are not, for module name and variable names.

Note allowed:

1. Any `.` anywhere in the module name, or parameter name, because it is not valid Python or SQL variable names.
2. `_`  as prefix. eg, `_simulate`, because R does not like it.
3. `_` as suffix, eg. `simulate_`, because it looks incomplete and ugly.
4. `-` in variable name, because it will not work in most languages
4. `_` followed by digit / digits as suffix, in module name, eg, `simulate_1`. This is because `_1`, an internal convention of index, will cause us troubles. The reason we pick `_{digit(s)}` as index is because it feels natural that way (eg, LaTeX). Also, since our automatic output file naming convention is with `_{digits}` it will create a minor issue there too.

Allowed:

1. `simulate_V2` or `simulate_2V` for module names.
2. `x_1`, `x_2` etc in variable names.

So the only restriction that we can possibly relax is `_{digits}` in module names.

We don't have enough information to create a VRID. For a meaningful VRID we'd need one or more floating ips (used for the NAT pool), priorities, and monitors and their priority costs.

There are basically two setups that use VRIDs: a failover pair that uses a single VRID or a chassis with multiple blades that uses `N*(N-1)` VRIDs where `N` is the number of blades in the chassis. A setting for a single default vrid supports the failover pair. In either case, the VRIDs, their priorities, monitors, and priority costs need to be configured.
Note that we should set blocked to True for these by default.

Doing this the right way requires changing an interface:

```go
type Backend interface {
	Create([]string, *sync.WaitGroup, chan string, chan error)
	Destroy([]string, *sync.WaitGroup, chan string, chan error)
	Start([]string, *sync.WaitGroup, chan string, chan error)
	Stop([]string, *sync.WaitGroup, chan string, chan error)
	Scale(string, int, *sync.WaitGroup, chan string, chan error)
	SSH(string) error
	ListUnits() error
	ListUnitFiles() error
	Status(string) error
	Journal(string) error
}
```

Changing exported interfaces without revving the major version number is generally considered bad form.

We can do one of the following:

- Create a second interface (`StateManager` or something) and re-implement the internals using the new backend, and then add a glue layer to expose the old interface, too. Lots of work.
- Create a second interface, and use that, but don't change (or use) the original one.
- Make a breaking change to the interface, on the theory that nobody outside of the project is actually using the `deisctl` code as a library, and hence nobody will care.
- Completely ignore the channels altogether, leave the interface alone, and add a new way to set the input streams.
- Delay fixing this until Deis 2, whenever that happens.

The part of PyCrypto you're using is the textbook/raw RSA, unfortunately that by itself isn't secure so you need to use a construction to ensure that the problems with raw RSA are worked around. This is actually in the documentation for the method that conda is using in the PyCrypto docs where they recommend the use of PSS or PKCS#1 v1.5 instead. My memory is a little foggy around those two, I think that v1.5 is "probably ok" but that PSS is the one you likely want to use if you're using RSA now. Things like DSA have similar stories if i recall correctly, where you need to make sure you use them in the correct way, and the correct way is often non-obvious and the wrong way is equally non-obvious.

PyCrypto unfortunately (sorry @dlitz!) follows the pattern of many crypto libraries where it gives you a box full of crypto legos and (mostly) leaves it up to you to figure out which legos are safe and in which combination. This isn't an uncommon situation to find yourself in and people using an otherwise secure algorithm in an insecure construct is the cause of a lot of CVEs.

As far as where to go from here, I want to first preface this with the statement that I am not a cryptographer and a lot of the math is beyond my abilities. I do however have a working knowledge of how to implement some parts of a secure cryptosystem (at least at a level higher than average). If you want to be really sure you're doing the right thing engaging a real expert is the best thing to do.

That being said, what I think you should do depends on what you're trying to achieve here. Signatures are great but there are a number of ways to attack a package repository even with signed packages where the signatures were done in a completely correct way. [TUF](http://theupdateframework.com/) is a system designed to handle securely fetching updates and it is my intent to integrate that with PyPI/pip once I get far enough down the stack to that point. It protects against a number of things like people blocking updates for a particular version of a library (say you have versions 1.0 and 1.0.1, where 1.0 is insecure but 1.0.1 has the fix, they'd block clients from seeing 1.0.1), or presenting the client with a inconstent state of the repository where the combination of packages may combine to be insecure, as well as other attacks. The folks behind it are friendly and helpful and I'm sure they'd be willing to help integrate it (It was a PhD project).

If you're not looking to protect against those kinds of attacks and you're just looking for something that will verify the files itself, then my first suggestion would be to throw away RSA completely and just use the signature support in NaCl. NaCl is a library written by an actual cryptography (Daniel J Bernstein) where the idea was that the interface of existing libraries make it too easy for people to get things wrong, so it won't let them do that. The biggest problem with NaCl is it uses "less standard" primitives (in particular, signing uses ed25519) which are still fairly standard and wide used but which might matter in some regulatory situations (such as if you're trying to comply with the FIPS 140-2 requirements of the United States Government). I'm *probably* going to use ed25519 in PyPI/pip because it produces deterministic signatures and the keys and signatures are quite small (64 byte signatures, 32 byte keys). The interface provided by the C library (which if you do, you want to use libsodium, not the original NaCl library) is similar to what you're using here, you get a simple sign/unsign API with the exception that it makes all the decisions for you, and those decisions were made by an actual cryptographer. There are a number of Python bindings to do this, including one I wrote called PyNaCl.

If for whatever reason you're not comfortable using ed25519 (which I would think long and hard before deciding I wasn't comfortable using it), I *think* that if you use RSA-PSS with at least 2048 bit keys (though I'd suggest larger if possible) then the actual "sign this file and verify it" part would be safe.

On top of all of that, the point that @coderanger made about needing more guidance about how to deal with trust is an important one as well. Even the most secure signature scheme is only as good as the trust model that is in play. The typical linux model is that the signing keys are associated with a particular repository, and if you trust that repository you generally trust it for *anything*. I don't know enough about conda to know if that is acceptable, but it's not for PyPI (because just because you trust me to sign a release for pip, that doesn't mean you trust me to sign a release for bokeh). TUF can help here if you need to be able to delegate portions of a single repository to different signing keys, but the simpler, "flat" model of the Linux repositories isn't wrong if each Conda repository is assumed to be owned by a single entity (be it a person, a project, or a company) and you generally assume you trust them entirely across the entire namespace.

Another problem with the current approach (which would persist even if you swapped out RSA for NaCl) is that these signature schemes have no concept of revocation or key expiration. That means you need to bake the idea into conda itself how you're going to handle the case where a key becomes compromised and how you handle that. A simple, but not particularly friendly, way is to just swap your singing key and tell everyone to manually update the signing keys to the new one in their conda installs. This is again something that TUF can help with.

I know I wrote a ton of words, so just to summarize:

1. If you can talk to the folks behind TUF and use TUF to secure conda, that would cover a lot of cases that you've probably not thought about and they've done a lot of the hardwork in designing a secure cryptosystem for package managers.
2. If you can't or don't want to use TUF, I would suggest ed25519 via NaCl with the understanding that there are going to be things you're not handling, but that the basic case of "verify this file was signed by this key" would be correct.
3. If you can't or don't want to use ed25519, then I would suggest RSA-PSS with at least 2048bit keys (but recommend larger) with the same understanding about things you're not handling from above.

Hopefully if anything I said is incorrect here, one of the other folks who have participated will point it out quickly :)

As also mentioned [here](https://github.com/shogun-toolbox/shogun/issues/4068), the error stems from the fact that pyBigWig is linked dynamically to python library, however the interpreter uses statically linked python. So, if `-undefined dynamic_lookup` linker flag is added (which is added by default, as far as I can see in my builds) and if `python3.6m` is not added to linked libraries, the error goes away. So I managed to make it work with following patch:

```patch
diff --git a/setup.py b/setup.py
index 479cd51..ee50ca5 100755
--- a/setup.py
+++ b/setup.py
@@ -16,15 +16,6 @@ srcs = [x for x in
 srcs.append("pyBigWig.c")

 libs=["m", "z", "curl"]
-if sysconfig.get_config_vars('BLDLIBRARY') is not None:
-    #Note the "-l" prefix!
-    for e in sysconfig.get_config_vars('BLDLIBRARY')[0].split():
-        if e[0:2] == "-l":
-            libs.append(e[2:])
-elif(sys.version_info[0] >= 3 and sys.version_info[1] >= 3) :
-    libs.append("python%i.%im" % (sys.version_info[0], sys.version_info[1]))
-else :
-    libs.append("python%i.%i" % (sys.version_info[0], sys.version_info[1]))

 additional_libs = [sysconfig.get_config_var("LIBDIR"), sysconfig.get_config_var("LIBPL")]
```

@daquang You are using conda-forge python on Mac, right? can you try building pyBigWig with this patch and check if it still works?

@suoto If we were to look for words like failure, fatal, warning, and error followed by a non-alphanumeric character and the assertion message includes any of these words without being a message of that severity we would have problems. The ModelSim message format could be changed to include such words even though the message being printed isn't of that level of severity but I can't see that this would be a major problem. I would expect notes like "error count: 0" or notes from an instance named `error_handler` to be a bigger problem. If that happens people can accept the false positive (the test case will still pass), change the sting/naming, use `--no-color`, or we could add configuration to control highlighting behaviour.

New or renamed log levels would be ignored but in general we can't make any assumptions on how such a level should be handled. For example, if someone renames `warning_low2` to `issue` we cannot know if that should be highlighted or not and what color to use. This has to be controlled by configuration.

The other logging solutions I'm referring to are primarily VHDL report/assert but also OSVVM and the Bitvis Utility Library (BVUL). We try to avoid lock-in situations where you have to use the VUnit solutions in order to have access to some features. Considering that the BVUL/OSVVM promoted way of doing testbenches involves logging more or less everything that happens and put that on stdout I'm imagining that they would benefit from coloring even more to easily spot the more interesting parts of long logs. They do not have the possibility to log the details to file (normally just interesting when debugging) while just showing more severe messages and progress information on stdout. Using the keyword I mentioned previously seems to work well for these libraries as well.

Btw, have you looked for already existing Python packages for we are trying to do?

Oh, I forgot the most important thing...

Because the conceptual model we are using is different from any other
selection UI I know of, the visual design is actually really difficult. As
of right now, I am not happy with anything we have proposed.

On Sat, Nov 21, 2015 at 11:45 AM, Brian Granger <ellisonbg@gmail.com> wrote:

> I am fine with "a visual treatment that is more pronounced and uses more
> color" - but simply coloring *everything* without any attempt to create a
> design that works well isn't a solution. I don't think it is a matter of
> picking a different color - to do that right we would need to change the
> DOM structure of a cell to carefully constraint which parts of highlighted.
>
> But more importantly, our conceptual model is completely different from
> both text editors and selection UIs such as Gmail. In both of those cases,
> actions *never* apply to the cursor. In our case, they *always* apply to
> the cursor. That super important fact isn't reflected in this design.
>
> The final point that I feel is important is that orthogonal concepts
> should be represented using orthogonal visual encodings in a minimal and
> consistent manner:
>
> * Solid blue backgrouns is being used to warn the user about which cells
> the action will apply to - but that encoding is not on the cursor cell
> where most actions will be applied to.
> * The cursor cell is encoded visually using both a grey border and the
> left blue thick border.
> * If you have large cells with lots of content, even selected a few of
> them will turn your entire screen blue (or another color). That is maximal
> visual encoding, not minimal.
> * The solid blue shading is so strong that it is really hard to see where
> your cursor cell. Tracking it is very important as some actions (Return for
> edit mode) will only apply to it.
>
> @Carrea - as an aside, it is really difficult to evaluate visual design
> decisions when animated. Animations are perfect for evaluating interactions
> and behaviors. Could you post a static image of the visual design along
> with the animation?
>
>

Hi @diegopacheco , sorry that is a bit confusing and we're working on making it less so.  The tokens under "Generate User API Access Token" are temporary tokens for talking with api.signalfx.com.  For ingestion you found the right ones under "Access Tokens".  

The reason your sample program isn't working is the timestamp is sent in milliseconds since epoch not seconds as python's time.time() function returns.  If you simply multiply by 1000 or omit it  completely and they will get the timestamp at the moment of ingestion. The problem is also the following: someone might have stored some data in a string, which is not exactly unicode. For example such a thing:
```
>>> (chr((0b110110 << 10) | 0b1010110011) + chr(0x0000)).encode("UTF-16", "surrogatepass").decode("UTF-16")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-16-le' codec can't decode bytes in position 2-3: illegal UTF-16 surrogate
```

So, the approach of decoding everything as UTF-16 works only if there are actual utf16 strings stored... This is exactly the problem with the given string:
0xd800 is treated as an high surrogate, but the following 0xfeff is not a low surrogate.

With Oscar in its current form, it's impossible to determine which images that are associated with a parent actually belong to which variants. For instance, you could have a T-shirt that comes in red, green and blue. In the cart, the best you can do is just grab the primary image, but that might not correspond to the correct color. A user might easily be confused if a red shirt appears in their cart when they added a green variant.

This PR adds a ManyToManyField that allows you to specify exactly which images go with which variant. This makes it trivial to select the correct image in the cart.

```        
# Optionals.
optionals = re.findall(RE.optionals, regexp)
for match in optionals:
    parts = match.split("|")
    new = []
    for p in parts:
        p = r'(?:\\s|\\b)+{}(?:\\s|\\b)+'.format(p)
        new.append(p)
```
This bug does no harm if we miss empty options. However if we want to place a check for empty string here, check_syntax function alone doesn't check this bug. This is only uncovered during the replacement of our symbols (i.e. wildcards by its corresponding Regex).

```python
location = pvlib.location.Location(32.229961, -110.95388, altitude=741, name='OASIS GHI', tz='US/Arizona')

# import not shown here
ghi = data.resample('1min', how='first')
cs = location.get_clearsky(ghi.index)

clear_samples = pvlib.clearsky.detect_clearsky(ghi, cs['ghi'], cs.index, 10)

cs['ghi'].plot(label='clear sky profile')
ghi.plot(label='measured ghi')
ghi[clear_samples].reindex(ghi.index).plot(label='detected clear')
plt.legend(ncol=3)
plt.ylabel('irradiance W/m**2')
plt.ylim(0, None)
plt.title(location.name)
```

Just my $0.02, but I think the design could be improved a lot if Layer was generalised to multiple inputs and multiple outputs, just like theano's graph.

You'd avoid special-case subclasses for MergeLayer (multiple inputs) and InputLayer (zero inputs) which have incompatible interfaces to the superclass (which assumes a single input) and require isinstance checks.

You could also handle things like get_outputs for multiple layers via wrapping them as a single layer with multiple outputs.

Making get_outputs etc methods on Layer would then make it a lot easier to implement composite layers that wrap assemblies of other layers. Currently doing this requires some nasty hackery.

Now that our test coverage and documentation are in a pretty good state, fixing up the regularization module is one of the last few things we need to do before we're ready for release. Some good ideas for a new API have been discussed in this thread. This discussion predates the `get_params()` API change and the introduction of tags though. I guess the main takeaway is that this module should operate on Theano expressions, not stacks of layers - and possibly provide one or more convenience functions that take a layer or list of layers as input. Although we'll have to see if that's actually useful at all (i.e. if it makes things significantly shorter and/or more readable).

We just need to turn this idea into a PR now :) Any takers?

Hi @timprepscius 
Something like:
```python
depth[disparity > 0] = (fx * baseline) / (units * disparity[disparity > 0])

fx          # lense focal length
baseline    # distance in mm between the two cameras
units       # depth units
```
(From [Depth from Stereo](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/depth-from-stereo.md))
Of course the secret sauce is finding the correct match with sub-pixel accuracy (disparity matrix can and should include floating point values, otherwise your depth will alway be quantised to set of discreet values)

Yeah, I'm definitely not crazy about it, but I have two issues with using fit.  The first is that using `fit` to determine qualitative identity of output data seems semantically counterintuitive to me.  If I'm "fitting" something, I'd imagine I'm determining quantitative model parameters from data.  It's not obvious to me that I'd need to use it to determine qualitative labels for output.  

The other issue is that it makes any featurizer that relies on input column identity to describe feature labels stateful in a way that makes it prone to both soft and hard errors.  For example, I might generate labels that correspond to a certain set of columns, and then reuse the featurize_dataframe method with different columns.  If the number of columns is different, this probably throws an error telling the user something vague about how two numbers aren't the same 5 layers into a pandas traceback, but if they use the correct number of columns (but different columns), then featurize dataframe works fine but labels all of the columns incorrectly.

```
class InstrumentedQuery(SQLAlchemyConnectionField):
    def __init__(self, type, *pos_args, **kwargs):
        self.query_args = {}
        kwargs['sort_by'] = String()

        for k, v in type._meta.fields.items():
            if isinstance(v, Field):
                field_type = v.type
                if isinstance(field_type, NonNull):
                    field_type = field_type.of_type
                self.query_args[k] = field_type()
        print(self.query_args)
        super().__init__(type, *pos_args, **kwargs, **self.query_args)

    def get_query(self, model, info, **args):
        query_filters = {k: v for k, v in args.items() if k in self.query_args}
        query = model.query.filter_by(**query_filters)
        if 'sort_by' in args:
            query.order_by(getattr(model, to_snake_case(args['sort_by'])))
        return query

    def connection_resolver(self, resolver, connection, model, root, info, **args):
        query = resolver(root, info, **args) or self.get_query(model, info, **args)
        count = query.count()
        connection = connection_from_list_slice(
            query,
            args,
            slice_start=0,
            list_length=count,
            list_slice_length=count,
            connection_type=connection,
            pageinfo_type=PageInfo,
            edge_type=connection.Edge,
        )
        connection.iterable = query
        connection.length = count
        return connection
```
